text: Three trocars are visibly placed in the patient's abdomen: one in the left upper quadrant, one in the left lateral abdomen, and one in the left lower quadrant, providing access for laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon uses a suction device to evacuate a significant amount of enteric content from the abdominal cavity. After evacuation, the focus shifts to the upper abdomen for further inspection.
The laparoscopic view reveals a small perforation of a pyloric channel ulcer located just beneath the surface of the liver, with the liver not fully covering the perforation.
The surgeon uses a suction device inserted into the perforation and an NG tube to evacuate enteric content, preventing oozing during the laparoscopic ulcer repair procedure.

The surgeon identifies a tongue of omentum and prepares it for use in the gram patch repair of the perforated gastric ulcer. Two sutures are simultaneously introduced into the abdominal cavity to secure the patch.

The surgeon uses a 2-0 silk stitch on an SH needle to place the first anchoring stitch above the pyloric channel ulcer perforation. The tongue of omentum is then fed underneath the stitch to cover the visible perforation.

The surgeon secures the suture over the tongue of omentum, ensuring minimal tension to prevent tearing the inflamed gastric wall. A figure of eight slip knot is created and tightened to achieve the appropriate tension.

The surgeon places a two-oh Vicryl stitch on an SH needle below the perforation, threading it over the tongue of omentum to secure the remaining stitch on the opposite side of the perforation. The use of different stitches aids in laparoscopic tying by allowing easy identification of suture ends.
The surgeon places a 10mm Jackson-Pratt drain around the patched perforation site to manage potential gastric outlet obstruction or any oozing from the repaired area.
